request.question: What type of criminals, named after a term from the Revolutionary War, were involved in the Clay County Savings Association Building robbery?

title: Clay County Savings Association Building
Edina Double Square Historic District
Bushwhacker
Clay County Courthouse (Alabama)
Claycomo, Missouri
Savings Bank Building
Old Clay County Courthouse (Clay, West Virginia)
Onondaga County Savings Bank
Clay County, Tennessee
Clay County Courthouse (Illinois)
sentences: The Clay County Savings Association in Liberty, Missouri, was robbed on February 13, 1866, making it one of the earliest documented daylight bank robberies.
The robbers escaped with at least $60,000 ( ) and killed a bystander outside.
The robbery is believed to have been conducted by a group of former Confederate guerrillas, possibly led by Jesse James or Archie Clement, which became known as the James–Younger Gang.
Edina Double Square Historic District is a national historic district located at Edina, Knox County, Missouri.
The district encompasses 37 contributing buildings in the central business district of Edina.
It developed between about 1865 and 1945 and includes representative examples of Italianate and Streamline Moderne style architecture.

Notable contributing buildings include the Public Works Administration funded Knox County Courthouse (1934-1935) designed by William B. Ittner, Bishoff Bakery (1891), Northern Hotel (1860s), Ennis House/Northern Hotel (c. 1865), Edina School and Gymnasium (1915-1916), D. H. Mudd Building (c. 1904), Phillip Linville Building (c. 1908, c. 1963), Tobias J. Lycan Building (c. 1891), Jacob Pugh Building (c. 1891), Albert G. Bostick Building (c. 1891), Knox County Savings Bank (c. 1891), Thomas Burk Buildings (c. 1881), Bank of Edina Building (c. 1907), Joseph F. Biggerstaff Buildings (c. 1881), Stablein Building (c. 1870), and Knox County Public Library (c. 1915).

Bushwhacking was a form of guerrilla warfare common during the American Revolutionary War, American Civil War and other conflicts in which there were large areas of contested land and few governmental resources to control these tracts.
This was particularly prevalent in rural areas during the Civil War where there were sharp divisions between those favoring the Union and Confederacy in the conflict.
The perpetrators of the attacks were called bushwhackers.
The term "bushwhacking" is still in use today to describe ambushes done with the aim of attrition.
The Clay County Courthouse is a historic courthouse building in Ashland, Clay County, Alabama.
The Classical Revival-style building has served as the county courthouse since its completion in 1906.
Notable characteristics include its large dome, with clocks inset on four sides, and cupola surmounted by a statuary representing justice.
It was added to the National Register of Historic Places on November 21, 1976.
Claycomo is a village in Clay County, Missouri, United States.
The village’s name is a portmanteau of "Clay, Co" (Clay County), and "Mo" (Missouri).
The population was 1,430 at the 2010 census.
Ford Motor Company has an assembly plant in Claycomo.
Kansas City Assembly is responsible for building the Ford F-150 pickup trucks and the Ford Transit vans.
The Savings Bank Building is a commercial building located at 101 South Front Street in Marquette, Michigan.
It is also known as the Marquette County Savings Bank.
The building was designated a Michigan State Historic Site in 1976 and listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1978.
The Old Clay County Courthouse in Clay, West Virginia was designed by Frank L. Packard and built in 1902.
The Beaux-Arts building was located on a hill overlooking the county seat.

The courthouse was the site of three notable trials: the Sarah Ann Legg trial of 1905, the first trial of a woman in Clay County for murder, the Booger Hole trial of 1917, in which citizens nearly lynched the defendants, and the Oscar Bail trial of 1953, in which Bail was convicted of killing a mine guard in the Great Widen Coal Strike.
The Onondaga County Savings Bank was chartered in 1855 in Syracuse, New York.
It was a franchise ahead of its time and had four separate branches by the late 19th century.
The "Onondaga County Savings Bank Building" was constructed in 1896 adjacent to the Erie Canal in "Clinton Square".
Clay County is a county located in the U.S. state of Tennessee.
As of the 2010 census, the population was 7,861.
Its county seat and only incorporated city is Celina.
Clay County is named in honor of American statesman Henry Clay, member of the United States Senate from Kentucky and United States Secretary of State in the 19th century.
Its current mayor is Dale Reagan.
The Clay County Courthouse, located at 300 Broadway Street in Louisville, is the county courthouse serving Clay County, Illinois.
Built in 1913, the courthouse was Clay County's fourth courthouse; it has served continuously as the seat of Clay County government since its opening.
Architect Joseph W. Royer, who planned several other Illinois courthouses, designed the Classical Revival building.
The courthouse was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 2015.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: What type of criminals, named after a term from the Revolutionary War, were involved in the Clay County Savings Association Building robbery?
hop1_docs: Clay County Savings Association Building | The Clay County Savings Association in Liberty, Missouri, was robbed on February 13, 1866, making it one of the earliest documented daylight bank robberies.  The robbers escaped with at least $60,000 ( ) and killed a bystander outside.  The robbery is believed to have been conducted by a group of former Confederate guerrillas, possibly led by Jesse James or Archie Clement, which became known as the James–Younger Gang.

Aryan Republican Army | The Aryan Republican Army is the name given to white supremacist criminal group active in the United States in the early to mid-1990s.  The group is alleged to have associated with convicted bomber Timothy McVeigh in the months before the Oklahoma City bombing.  The organization was sometimes referenced in the media as the Midwest Bank Robbers.  The group was created by Peter Langan and Richard Lee Guthrie in 1992.  The group considered themselves a leaderless organization, meaning that there was no true leader to the group and that they were more of a group of associated people acting together to achieve their goals.  Although the group was mainly a criminal enterprise, they did have a terrorist like agenda.  With the money they were able to steal in the 22 confirmed robberies they were involved in, the group began stockpiling weapons and ammunition.  It is believed that the group wished to start a race war with the weapons that were stockpiled.  The group began to fall apart as members of the Aryan Republican Army were arrested after one of the former members of the group became an informant as part of a plea bargain.  By mid-1996, practically all known members had been apprehended and the group was in shambles.

State Line Mob | The State Line Mob was an association of criminal elements that operated in the 1950s and 1960s at the Mississippi–Tennessee state line in Alcorn County, Mississippi, and McNairy County, Tennessee, along U.S. Route 45.  The State Line Mob was involved in bootlegging, gambling, prostitution, tourist fleecing, robbery, and murder.  A few of the members were from Phenix City, Alabama, having been displaced from that town when martial law was declared by the Governor and the Alabama National Guard attempted to clean the town up.

Florencia 13 | The Florencia 13 is an American criminal street gang in Los Angeles, California, composed mainly of Hispanic-Americans and some blacks.  The gangs name is after the Florence area of Los Angeles county, the 13 is for it's allegiance to the Mexican Mafia.  They are involved in drug smuggling, murder, assault, robbery etc.

Arifs (gang) | The Arifs are a South East London-based Turkish Cypriot criminal organization heavily involved in armed robbery, contract killing, drug trafficking and other racketeering-related activities within London's underworld since the late 1960s.  Following the downfall of the Kray brothers, the Arifs were one of several criminal organizations who took control of the London underworld including the Clerkenwell crime syndicate and the Brindle family with whom they were engaged in a highly publicized gangland war during the 1990s.

Quaker Springs, New York | Quaker Springs is a hamlet in Saratoga County.  The zip code is 12871.  Quaker Springs is between NY-32 Route 4.  Quaker Springs United Methodist Church is located in the hamlet.  An anti-slavery society was based in Quaker Springs and several possible participants in the Underground Railroad were in Quaker Springs.  The name Quaker Springs came from a type of mineral spring located in the area.  The area including Quaker Springs was involved in Revolutionary War positions.
U.S. intelligence involvement with German and Japanese war criminals after World War II | U.S. intelligence involvement with German and Japanese war criminals after World War II
hop1_titles: U.S. intelligence involvement with German and Japanese war criminals after World War II
response.summary_1:
- Passage [1]: "Clay County Savings Association in Liberty, Missouri, was robbed on February 13, 1866" (quoted).
- Passage [1]: robbers "escaped with at least $60,000" (quoted).
- Passage [1]: robbers "killed a bystander outside." (quoted).
- Passage [1]: robbery "believed to have been conducted by a group of former Confederate guerrillas" (quoted).
- Passage [1]: robbers were "possibly led by Jesse James or Archie Clement" (paraphrase).
- Passage [1]: the group "became known as the James–Younger Gang." (quoted).
- No other passage links the robber description to any specific Revolutionary‑War term (absence of evidence in passages [2]–[7]).

Bridge entities and ambiguities preventing a definitive answer:
- Bridge entity: the robbers of the Clay County Savings Association (identified in passage [1] as "former Confederate guerrillas").
- Ambiguity 1: The question asks for "criminals, named after a term from the Revolutionary War" — passages do not state which Revolutionary‑War term is meant (e.g., "guerrillas", "bushwhackers", "partisans", etc.). It is unclear whether the intended name equals the quoted "former Confederate guerrillas" or a different label.
- Ambiguity 2: Multiple related pages are referenced but not supplied (James–Younger Gang, Jesse James, Archie Clement) so no corroborating label/etymology is present.

Prioritized next-hop retrieval targets (what to fetch and what to extract):
1) Fetch the full "Clay County Savings Association Building" (or Clay County Savings Association) page — extract the lead and "Robbery" or "History" section text and any phrasing that names the robbers (look for specific label/term and any etymology note).
2) Fetch the "James–Younger Gang" page — extract the lead/Origins/Name sections and any descriptors (keywords: "bushwhackers", "guerrillas", "partisans", "name origin", "etymology").
3) Fetch the "Jesse James" and "Archie Clement" pages — extract lead/biography sections for labels used to describe them (keywords: "bushwhacker", "guerrilla", "Confederate guerrilla", "Missouri guerrilla").
4) Fetch pages on candidate terms' origins: "Bushwhacker", "Guerrilla warfare", and "Partisan (military)" — extract etymology/origin/usage sections to verify which term traces to the Revolutionary War (keywords: "Revolutionary War", "origin", "etymology", "usage in American Revolutionary War").
5) If a disambiguation exists for the questioned term (e.g., "bushwhacker (disambiguation)"), fetch it to confirm common historical usage.

Do not attempt a final answer yet: the passages provide "former Confederate guerrillas" (passage [1]) but do not unambiguously show which Revolutionary‑War term the question refers to.
response.hop2_query: Clay County Savings Association robbers "bushwhackers" name origin Revolutionary War
hop2_docs: Clay County Savings Association Building | The Clay County Savings Association in Liberty, Missouri, was robbed on February 13, 1866, making it one of the earliest documented daylight bank robberies.  The robbers escaped with at least $60,000 ( ) and killed a bystander outside.  The robbery is believed to have been conducted by a group of former Confederate guerrillas, possibly led by Jesse James or Archie Clement, which became known as the James–Younger Gang.

Bushwhacker | Bushwhacking was a form of guerrilla warfare common during the American Revolutionary War, American Civil War and other conflicts in which there were large areas of contested land and few governmental resources to control these tracts.  This was particularly prevalent in rural areas during the Civil War where there were sharp divisions between those favoring the Union and Confederacy in the conflict.  The perpetrators of the attacks were called bushwhackers.  The term "bushwhacking" is still in use today to describe ambushes done with the aim of attrition.

Pulaski County, Arkansas | Pulaski County is a county in the U.S. state of Arkansas with a population of 392,664, making it the most populous county in Arkansas.  Its county seat is Little Rock, which is also Arkansas's capital and largest city.  Pulaski County is Arkansas's fifth county, formed on December 15, 1818, alongside Clark and Hempstead counties.  The county is named for Count Casimir Pulaski, a Polish volunteer who saved George Washington's life during the American Revolutionary War.

Ben-Ishmael Tribe | The names "Ben-Ishmael Tribe," and "Tribe of Ishmael", were applied to poor, Upland Southern residents of Indianapolis, Indiana during the late 19th century because of their supposed association with the Ishmael family.  Records of the Ishmael family show that it originally hailed from Cumberland County, Pennsylvania, and that its patriarch, Benjamin Ishmael, served in the American Continental Army during the Revolutionary War.

Edward Sneyd Clay | Edward Sneyd Clay (c. 1768 – 3 February 1846) was an officer of the Royal Navy who served during the American War of Independence, and the French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars.

Pine Robbers | "Pine Robbers" were loosely organized, criminal gangs, and marauders who were British sympathizers and Loyalists, during the American Revolutionary War, who used the Pine Barrens of New Jersey to wreak havoc in the area.  The pine barrens created densely forested, terrain, where concealment of guerrilla and criminal activities, could easily be carried out.

Clay County, Iowa | Clay County is a county located in the U.S. state of Iowa.  As of the 2010 census, the population was 16,667.  Its county seat is Spencer.  Its name is in honor of Henry Clay, Jr., a colonel who died in action in the Mexican-American War, and son of Henry Clay, famous American statesman.
response.summary_2: They were former Confederate guerrillas. James–Younger Gang became the name of that group implicated in the February 13, 1866, Clay County Savings Association robbery. Bushwhacker refers to perpetrators of a form of guerrilla warfare that was common during the American Revolutionary War (and later the Civil War), and such fighters were called "bushwhackers."
